note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-11-01-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) predicts 236.993s but the ffprobed master is 236.513s (0.48s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 14 blocks concatenated with an exact 0.400s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.034s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates to -0.48s, which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (every onset landed on the 0.08s mp3-frame grid); final end = ffprobed master duration 236.513s. Verbatim text preserves the script spellings 'S-M-S' and 'Enth Wash Rewards' (spoken-pronunciation forms in the locked script).

phrases.text: Messaging costs money, and this is the page where that money shows up.
From the Billing area, open Usage and Billing.
One screen turns a wash's texts and emails into a bill.
When a client calls about messaging charges, everything you need is here,
and all of it is read only.
Read the line under the heading first.
Current billing period, May 2026, with the date range,
the next invoice date, and the days remaining.
Every number on this page belongs to that period.
Anchor here before you quote a single figure to a client.
Now the four tiles.
Two of them are spend: what texting cost this period, and what email cost.
The other two are volume, each with a delivered percentage beside it.
Cost on the left, activity on the right.
That's the whole bill at a glance.
Under the band, six tabs.
Overview is the summary, and it's open now.
The rest drill into one thing each: text detail, email detail, invoices,
the rate card, and spending alerts.
Notice the badge on Invoices.
One open.
That's a client's actual bill, waiting.
Start with the left panel, Monthly Usage Cost.
Stacked bars show combined text and email spend by month, back through November.
The current month carries an asterisk: estimated, month not complete.
Never quote May as final until it closes.
And one caption quirk: the subtitle says six months, but seven bars render.
Count the bars.
The right panel is the one to learn: Usage by Source.
Every row is one campaign, automation, or transactional stream,
with its volume, its cost, and a share bar ranking it.
This table answers the most common billing call there is: why did my bill move?
Read the rows top down.
This period, the Enth Wash Rewards automation is the biggest line at nearly thirty two dollars,
ahead of the membership drive and the win-back campaigns.
Receipts barely register.
One row usually explains the whole swing, and the share bars point you straight at it.
Scroll to the Year to Date Summary.
Platform fees, text and email cost for the year, total billed, and total paid,
with a checkmark reading current.
Billed equals paid, so this account is square.
There's an export control on this panel.
Leave it alone; nothing exports in this walk.
Now walk the tabs.
Click S-M-S Usage.
This is the detail behind the period's texting spend:
one row per send batch, with its recipients, deliveries, failures, and cost.
When a client asks whether a text actually went out,
the failed column against delivered is your answer.
Next, Email Usage.
Same log for the email channel, with bounces in place of failures.
Receipts show up here too, tagged transactional, at a fraction of a cent each.
Click Invoices, the tab with the badge.
Usage explains the charges; this tab holds the bill itself.
When a client wants the document rather than the why, send them here.
One invoice sits open in this period.
Rate Card next.
The multiplier that turns volume into spend:
two and a half cents a text, a fraction of a cent an email, and failed sends are never billed.
And Spending Alerts.
Thresholds that warn before messaging spend runs away,
with an early warning at eighty percent and a per-campaign cap that pauses sends over budget.
Worth knowing when a client is surprised by a bill.
Back to Overview.
Two questions cover most billing calls.
Why did the bill move?
Usage by Source, top row first.
Where's the bill itself?
The Invoices tab, badged one open.
Read the period line, respect the asterisk, and never touch the export.
That's the whole page.
